steps.title: Nowhere to Go
steps.text:
A piece deep in enemy territory is brave but vulnerable. **Trap it** — control every square it can flee to — and you win it without spending material.

## What makes a piece trappable

- It is **far from home**, with no friendly support.
- Its **mobility** is limited (a knight on the rim, a bishop blocked by pawns).
- It is the most valuable piece in its corner of the board (often a queen).
- The **attacker** has nearby pieces or pawns that can cover its retreats.

## The geometry

List every square the trapped piece can move to. If you can attack one of them or block another with each of your moves — without losing tempo — the piece dies one square at a time.

## How to spot it

1. Look for an enemy piece on **the wrong side of the board**.
2. Count the squares it can move to.
3. For each escape square, find a piece or pawn of yours that controls or blocks it.
4. Approach with a tempo move (often a check or a threat) so the trap is sealed before the piece can flee.

Here the black queen on d5 sits in front of her king with white rooks doubled on the e- and f-files. White can play moves that systematically take away every square the queen can reach.

> A queen far from home is just an expensive prisoner.

steps.title: Rim Knights and Trapped Rooks
steps.text:
Two of the most common trapping motifs you will see in real games:

## The rim knight

A knight on the **a-** or **h-file** controls only four squares instead of the usual eight. Push pawns at it. Block its retreats. Bring a piece next to it. Game over.

- **a4 + b5** vs. a Black knight on a6 → the knight has no good square.
- A bishop on c8 hemmed in by its own pawns, then attacked by a knight on a7.

> *"A knight on the rim is dim."* — Tarrasch

## The trapped rook

Rooks deep in enemy territory chasing a pawn (a2, h2) often get caught.

- The rook captures a pawn on a2.
- The opponent's king walks to b1.
- A piece (often a bishop or knight) covers the escape square.
- The rook is gone.

## How to find the trap

1. Identify the **far-from-home** enemy piece.
2. Count its escape squares.
3. Use **tempo moves** (checks, threats) to bring attackers in without giving the piece a chance to flee.

> Adventurous pieces need an adventurous owner — and an unobservant opponent.
